section.title: My Heart Is Steadfast, O God
section.sectionRationale: The opening psalm of Book V's central Davidic cluster, this song stitches together the praise of Psalm 57 and the petition of Psalm 60, showing how God's people gather up old prayers and sing them again in a new hour of need.
section.thesis: A heart made steadfast by God's love that reaches beyond the heavens can praise before the battle is won, and can ask boldly for help because human strength was always worthless without Him.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a steadfast heart resolves to praise among the nations, verses 1-5; God speaks from His sanctuary and claims the land as His own, verses 6-9; and Israel asks for aid against the enemy, confident that with God alone comes valor, verses 10-13.

verses.text: A song. A Psalm of David. My heart is {{kun|steadfast}}, O God; I will sing and make music with all my being.
Awake, O harp and lyre! I will awaken the dawn.
I will praise You, O LORD, among the nations; I will sing Your praises among the peoples.
For Your {{chesed|loving devotion}} extends beyond the heavens, and Your faithfulness reaches to the clouds.
Be exalted, O God, above the heavens; may Your glory cover all the earth.

units.label: God has spoken from His sanctuary

verses.text: Respond and save us with Your right hand, that Your beloved may be delivered.
God has spoken from His sanctuary: "I will triumph! I will parcel out Shechem and apportion the Valley of Succoth.
Gilead is Mine, and Manasseh is Mine; Ephraim is My helmet, Judah is My scepter.
Moab is My washbasin; upon Edom I toss My sandal; over Philistia I shout in triumph."

units.label: Give us aid against the enemy
units.note: human help is worthless, God is not

verses.text: Who will bring me to the fortified city? Who will lead me to Edom?
Have You not rejected us, O God? Will You no longer march out, O God, with our armies?
Give us aid against the enemy, for the help of man is worthless.
With God we will perform with {{chayil|valor}}, and He will trample our enemies.

kun.gloss: Firm, fixed, established. David's heart is not wavering between doubt and faith but settled, ready to praise before the outcome of the battle is known.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant love and loyalty. This love is pictured as vaster than the heavens themselves, the ground of both praise, verses 1-5, and petition, verses 6-13.

chayil.gloss: Strength, valor, capability for battle. The psalm insists this valor is never self-generated; it comes only through God, who alone tramples the enemy.

basic.context: David stitches together an old song of praise, Psalm 57, and an old song of petition, Psalm 60, into one new psalm for a fresh crisis, likely a struggle over Edom. The pattern teaches that yesterday's worship can fuel today's prayer.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm moves from resolved praise, to God's own claim over the land, to a bold request for help, showing that confident worship and honest need are not opposites but companions in a life of faith.
basic.originalLanguages: David's heart is kun, steadfast, fixed, not wavering. God's chesed, His covenant love, is said to reach beyond the heavens. And the victory Israel needs is chayil, valor, which the psalm insists comes only through God, never through man.
basic.typology: David as king recites God's claim over the nations and pleads for triumph over every enemy; this anticipates great David's greater Son, whose kingdom truly extends over Shechem and Edom and every nation, and who tramples the last enemy, death itself.
basic.christ: The King greater than David inherits every promise this psalm voices: the nations belong to Him, Psalm 2:8; He marches out for His people's deliverance; and in Him alone, not in any human help, His people find true and final victory over every foe, culminating in His triumph over death and the grave.
basic.god: God's loving devotion is not confined to Israel's borders but towers over the heavens and reaches every cloud. He speaks with sovereign authority over lands His people do not yet possess, and He alone, not human strength, secures real victory.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 57:7-11 supplies the praise; Psalm 60:5-12 supplies the petition. Romans 8:31 echoes the psalm's confidence that with God for us, no one can stand against us. Revelation 11:15 pictures the final fulfillment of God's claim over every kingdom of the earth.
basic.oneStory: A steadfast heart praises before the victory arrives because it trusts the God whose love is higher than the heavens; this same confidence carries forward to Christ, in whom every nation is finally claimed and every enemy, even death, is trampled underfoot.

context.literary: A composite psalm: verses 1-5 draw from Psalm 57:7-11 and verses 6-13 from Psalm 60:5-12, joined to form a single new song of praise and petition for a later occasion.
context.historical: The petition for aid against Edom recalls the wars recorded in 2 Samuel 8 and 1 Chronicles 18, when Israel sought territory east and south of the Jordan and faced setbacks that only God's intervention could reverse.
context.canonical: Standing at the head of Book V's cluster of Davidic psalms, 108 through 110, it models how earlier praise is remembered and repurposed, a pattern that anticipates how the whole Psalter is prayed and re-prayed by later generations, including the church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To fuse remembered praise with present petition, teaching that thanksgiving for God's greatness and urgent prayer for God's help belong together, not as contradictory postures but as one steadfast heart's full expression.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific military crisis over Edom; it prescribes the abiding pattern of resolved praise preceding and sustaining bold petition, and of trusting God's strength rather than human help in every conflict.

debates.question: Why does a psalm of confident praise, verses 1-5, turn immediately to a plea that seems to admit past rejection, verse 11?

views.label: The psalm holds together settled faith and honest lament without contradiction
views.case: Hebrew prayer regularly moves between praise and complaint in the same breath; verse 11 names present hardship even as verses 1-5 have already resolved to trust and praise.

views.label: Verses 6-13, borrowed from Psalm 60, retain their original tone of crisis even though verses 1-5 were composed in a season of settled worship
views.case: Because the psalm is a splice of two earlier compositions, the shift in tone reflects its composite origin rather than a single unified emotional arc.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm teaches that steadfast praise and urgent petition are not sequential stages to move past, but a single posture of faith that can hold both at once.

study.originalLanguages: David's heart is kun, fixed and unwavering, able to praise before the outcome is certain. God's chesed reaches beyond the heavens. And the chayil, the valor, Israel needs for victory comes only through God, never through the worthless help of man.

typology.type: The king who receives the nations as inheritance, verses 7-9
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in great David's greater Son, to whom the Father says, Ask of Me, and I will make the nations Your heritage, Psalm 2:8, ultimately realized when every kingdom of the world becomes the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, Revelation 11:15.

typology.type: Valor and victory that come only through God, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's triumph over every enemy, culminating in His conquest of death itself, 1 Corinthians 15:57, a victory in which His people share only because it is His, not their own.

study.christ: David's claim that the nations, Shechem, Gilead, Manasseh, Ephraim, Judah, and even hostile Edom and Philistia, belong to God anticipates the greater King to whom the Father grants the nations as an inheritance, Psalm 2:8. The psalm's insistence that human help is worthless and that valor comes only through God finds its fullest answer in Christ, who alone secures the victory His people could never win for themselves, trampling sin, death, and every hostile power through His cross and resurrection.
study.god: God's covenant love is not parochial; it extends beyond the heavens and covers all the earth. He speaks with sovereign authority, distributing lands as though they were already His own possession, and He alone, not human strength or alliance, grants real and lasting victory to His people.

crossReferences.note: Supplies the psalm's opening praise almost verbatim, showing how earlier worship is remembered and repurposed.
Supplies the psalm's closing petition, rooted in an earlier crisis over Edom.
Echoes the psalm's confidence that God for us guarantees the outcome no human opposition can overturn.
The final, complete fulfillment of God's claim over every nation and kingdom.

study.oneStory: A steadfast heart can praise before the victory is seen because God's covenant love towers over the heavens; the same voice that pleads for help against Edom ultimately points forward to Christ, in whom every nation is claimed and every enemy, even death, is trampled underfoot by God's own valor, not man's.

quiz.q: What does David resolve to do even before the outcome of the battle is known?

options.text: Praise God with a steadfast heart, singing among the nations.
options.note: Verses 1-3 show praise preceding and sustaining the petition that follows.

options.text: Wait until victory is certain before offering any praise.
options.note: The opposite is true; David's heart is already steadfast in praise.

options.text: Seek help from Edom's allies first.
options.note: The psalm explicitly rejects human help, verse 12.

options.text: Remain silent until God explains His plan.
options.note: David is not silent; he sings and makes music immediately.

quiz.q: According to verse 4, how vast is God's loving devotion?

options.text: It extends beyond the heavens, and His faithfulness reaches to the clouds.
options.note: This cosmic scale grounds the psalm's confident praise.

options.text: It is limited to the borders of Israel.
options.note: The psalm describes love reaching beyond the heavens, not confined to any border.

options.text: It depends on Israel's obedience that week.
options.note: The psalm grounds love in God's own character, not in performance.

options.text: It is measured only in military victories.
options.note: The love described is cosmic in scope, not merely tied to battle outcomes.

quiz.q: In verses 7-9, on what basis does God claim Shechem, Gilead, Manasseh, and even Moab and Edom?

options.text: He speaks from His sanctuary, asserting sovereign ownership over them all.
options.note: God's own oracle claims both Israelite territory and hostile nations as His.

options.text: Israel had already fully conquered every one of these regions.
options.note: The psalm's plea for aid in verses 10-13 shows the conquest is not yet complete.

options.text: David purchased the land through treaties.
options.note: The claim is God's own sovereign declaration, not a political treaty.

options.text: The nations volunteered to submit to Israel.
options.note: The oracle is God's assertion of ownership, not a voluntary surrender by the nations.

quiz.q: What does the psalm say about the value of human help in verse 12?

options.text: The help of man is worthless.
options.note: This blunt statement redirects all trust toward God alone.

options.text: Human help is sufficient if enough allies are gathered.
options.note: The text says the opposite; human help is called worthless.

options.text: Human help matters more than divine help.
options.note: The psalm reverses this priority entirely.

options.text: Human help is useful only in defensive wars.
options.note: The verse makes no such distinction; it flatly calls human help worthless.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's vision of the nations point toward Christ?

options.text: It anticipates the greater King to whom the Father grants all nations as an inheritance, fulfilled ultimately when every kingdom becomes Christ's.
options.note: Psalm 2:8 and Revelation 11:15 complete what this psalm foreshadows.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The psalm's claim over the nations directly anticipates Christ's universal reign.

options.text: It predicts the exact modern borders of Israel.
options.note: The psalm's scope is about God's sovereign claim, not modern political geography.

options.text: It shows that only ethnic Israel will ever belong to God.
options.note: The psalm's vision widens, in the whole canon, to every nation belonging to Christ.

questions: David's heart is steadfast enough to praise before the victory is won. What would it look like for you to praise God now, in the middle of an unresolved struggle, rather than waiting until it is over?
The psalm insists that the help of man is worthless compared to God's help. Where are you currently leaning on human strength or strategy in a way that quietly competes with trusting God?
God's loving devotion is described as reaching beyond the heavens. How does meditating on the sheer scale of God's love change the size of the problem you are currently facing?
David remembers and repurposes old songs of praise and petition for a new crisis. What has God done in your past that you could remember and pray again over a present difficulty?

This psalm ultimately points to a King who receives the nations as His inheritance and tramples every enemy. How does knowing that Christ has already secured final victory shape the way you face today's opposition?

translationNotes.note: Steadfast renders the Hebrew kun, a heart that is fixed or established, not wavering between fear and faith.
The oracle in verses 7-9 is spoken in God's own first-person voice from His sanctuary, claiming territories as legal possessions rather than merely describing a future hope.
Valor renders chayil, the same term used for military strength; the psalm insists this strength is granted by God rather than generated by human effort.

Verified 13 verses in order against the section map bounds Psalm 108:1-13. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was confirmed for this specific psalm this session, so sermon status is none.
